Deloitte●
Product & Delivery Analyst
Client: HCSC
Dec 2022 – Aug 2025
Bangalore, India
I started as a developer on a legacy healthcare claims platform for HCSC — writing code, debugging pipelines, understanding how the system actually worked under the hood. About a year in, I moved into agile delivery and product for the same platform. Knowing the system from the inside changed how I approached every requirement, every tradeoff, every stakeholder conversation. That's just how it worked out for me, and honestly it shaped how I think about product to this day.

Over 2.5 years, I was recognized with the Deloitte Applause Award three times — an internal recognition given for consistently delivering high-quality outcomes and driving measurable client impact.
Development Analyst
→Developed and maintained 40+ Spring Boot components for the claims adjudication platform, supporting automated deployments through UCD-driven CI/CD pipelines
→Identified and resolved 100+ high-priority UAT defects across the claims processing pipeline, improving system reliability ahead of production releases
JavaSpring BootCI/CDUATClaims Modernisation
Product & Delivery Analyst
→Identified and drove delivery of a workflow traceability feature supporting 17M+ members, reducing manual troubleshooting by 80% across 10,000+ claims — earned Deloitte Applause Award
→Conducted SQL-based root-cause analysis on incorrect claim denials, refining eligibility logic and acceptance criteria to reduce wrongful rejections by 60%
→Translated business requirements into 360+ user stories and acceptance criteria across 5+ workstreams, driving feature prioritization that improved claims accuracy from 95% to 98%
→Performed sprint-level analytics in JIRA tracking backlog trends, velocity, and defect leakage — maintaining ~90% on-time, scope-aligned delivery
→Presented 25+ product demos to 30+ VP-level stakeholders, translating complex claims logic into clear narratives that informed release decisions
→Coordinated sprint planning, backlog refinement, retrospectives, and standups across 5+ workstreams; developed meeting agendas, captured minutes, and maintained action registers aligned to PMO standards

01 · VALIDATED
Align
Align is a group scheduling platform built for students. It syncs with Canvas, automatically detects free windows across the group, and sends behavioral nudges to get everyone to commit. No more chasing. No more group chats going silent.
30+
Interviews
78%
Intent-to-use
50+
Validated
Problem
→73% of students say coordination is the hardest part of group work. The problem is not finding a time. It is getting everyone to respond.
→Every group project has one person who ends up sending reminders, following up, and chasing responses. Align removes that person from the equation entirely by automating the coordination layer.
→Existing tools like Doodle or When2meet require manual input and have no follow-up mechanism. Align pulls schedules directly from Canvas and handles the rest.
Approach
→Conducted 30+ student interviews, synthesized 3 personas spanning organizer, participant, and recurring-user workflows
→Designed a 3-stage behavioral nudge system using loss aversion, social proof, and deadline pressure
→Built a 10-screen React MVP with Canvas schedule ingestion, conflict detection, and ranked slot recommendations
Outcome
→78% intent-to-use validated with 50+ Tufts students — highest signal for a go/no-go decision
→Shipped a fully functional 10-screen React MVP — not a prototype, a working product with real Canvas ingestion, conflict detection, and ranked slot output
→Auto-scheduling logic built in — once a common slot is found, the organizer chooses between majority quorum or full-group consensus before confirming
→Behavioral nudge system live — 3-stage escalation fires automatically for non-responders, removing the need for any manual follow-up
→Full product documentation delivered: PRD, persona decks, wireframes across 15+ screens, and usability test report
Takeaways
→Some problems are so small nobody talks about them. Coordination anxiety in group projects was one. Spotting that gap was the whole product.
→Latent needs do not announce themselves. You find them in the gap between what users say they do and what actually happens when you watch them.
→Rough prototypes beat polished ones early. v0.1 exposed the right problems fast. Every flaw in it directly shaped what v1.0 prioritised.
→The best feature was not scheduling. It was removing the decision entirely. Ranked slots meant users did not have to choose, they just had to confirm.
User ResearchCustomer DiscoveryBehavioral DesignPersona DevelopmentWireframing and PrototypingReactUsability TestingRequirements DefinitionFeature PrioritizationA/B TestingOKRs and KPIs
02 · DISCOVERED
EasyDry
EasyDry is a commercial-grade smart shoe drying station built for offices, hotels, and gyms. Proprietary air-jet insert technology dries shoes in 15–30 minutes. Positioned under SharkNinja (Shark brand).
40+
Interviews
$499
Price gap
4
Segments
Problem
→Wet footwear costs commercial facilities $1,000+/month in cleaning labor — slippery floors, tracked dirt, and no fast fix
→Existing shoe dryers take 3+ hours and are built for home use — completely unfit for high-traffic shared spaces
→52% of surveyed users expect a shoe dryer at work. 50% of users just deal with wet shoes. 95% prefer dry shoes over clean shoes.
Approach
→Co-led 40+ user interviews and surveys across facility managers and employees to validate problem-market fit and segment the ICP
→Developed 2 original product concepts with annotated sketches; identified the shoe insert with air-jet nozzles as the core patentable mechanism
→Performed competitive analysis across 4 solutions, finding an uncontested $499 price gap between $300 and $800 competitors
→Co-authored the Preliminary Business Plan including financial model, 5-year projections, and marketing strategy
→Positioned under SharkNinja (Shark brand) to leverage manufacturing credibility and reduce B2B adoption friction
Product concept & market positioning
Outcome
→Investor-style pitch co-presented to a panel — covering product innovation, ICP, competitive positioning, and financial projections
→Full GTM roadmap across 4 target segments: offices, hotels, gyms, and commercial facilities — with channel strategy, pricing model, and distribution approach
→Preliminary Business Plan delivered with 5-year financial model and marketing strategy
→Product concept validated with $499 price point sitting in an uncontested market gap — deliberate, not accidental
Takeaways
→Start with users, not ideas. 40+ interviews gave concrete data to anchor every deliverable — market sizing, projections, and the pitch all became more credible because of it.
→Whitespace pricing is a strategy. $499 sits in an uncontested gap between $300 and $800 competitors — deliberate, not accidental.
→B2B means two audiences. The buyer (facility manager) and user (employee) have different motivations. Marketing had to speak to both separately.
→Name your IP early. Identifying the shoe insert as a patentable mechanism gave the team a clear answer to "what stops a competitor from copying this?"
→Parent company framing reduces friction. SharkNinja gave EasyDry manufacturing credibility and brand trust that a standalone concept would have lacked in a B2B pitch.
B2B DiscoveryUser InterviewsCompetitive AnalysisGTM StrategyPricing Strategy
03 · PRODUCT MARKETING
VetMotl
VetMotl makes veterinary sperm separation devices for assisted reproduction (ART). I worked as a marketing consultant to revise their value proposition grounded in primary research, identifying gaps in the ART competitive landscape and building audience-specific messaging for equine and bovine reproduction specialists.
Veterinary ART Market Primary Research Competitive Analysis Value Proposition Design HubSpot Campaigns
Problem
→VetMotl had a technically differentiated product but lacked a clear value proposition for its target buyers — equine and bovine reproduction specialists and embryologists
→The ART veterinary market is highly niche and relationship-driven. Generic marketing doesn't work. The strategy had to be grounded in how practitioners actually make purchasing decisions.
Approach
→Conducted primary research through structured interviews and a Qualtrics survey targeting equine reproduction specialists and embryologists to identify buyer pain points and decision criteria
→Performed competitive analysis across the ART veterinary landscape — mapping gaps in positioning, pricing, and product differentiation
→Identified must-have customer value propositions for VetMotl's sperm separation devices across specific buyer segments
→Developed and executed HubSpot email campaigns targeting prospect segments identified through research
Product & competitive analysis
Outcome
→Landscape Analysis Report covering market size, IVF adoption, genetics economics, on-farm feasibility, competitive landscape, and marketing recommendations
→Blog post translating primary research into content for progressive dairy producers, ready for VetMotl's website or partner publications
→Social media strategy with concrete positioning, targeting, and messaging recommendations grounded in landscape data
→HubSpot email campaigns designed and executed for segmented prospect outreach
Takeaways
→In niche B2B markets, research is the strategy. Without primary data from actual practitioners, any positioning would have been a guess.
→Value propositions need to be audience-specific. What matters to an embryologist is different from what matters to a farm owner. One message does not fit both.
→Competitive gaps are not always about price. The biggest gap in the ART landscape was clarity. Most competitors could not explain what made them better in terms buyers actually used.
→In science-driven markets, the product has to be backed by science too. Adoption resistance in ART came from practitioners not trusting new methods over established ones. White papers and evidence-based messaging were not optional — they were the product story.
Product MarketingPrimary ResearchCompetitive AnalysisGo-to-Market StrategyHubSpotEmail CampaignsCustomer DiscoveryValue Proposition DesignQualtricsContent Strategy
04 · PROTOTYPED & PUBLISHED
Self-Driving Shopping Cart
A self-driving shopping cart prototype designed to enable visually impaired users to shop independently. Built on Raspberry Pi and Arduino Uno with computer vision, machine learning, and deep neural networks. Recognized at Yukti 2.0 by the Ministry of Education, Govt. of India, and published in Springer 2023.
Yukti 2.0 Winner Springer 2023 Ministry of Education, Govt. of India View Publication ↗
Problem
→Visually impaired people have no way to shop independently. They rely entirely on friends, family, or shopping assistants to navigate retail spaces.
→Existing systems (RFID carts, line-following robots) require human pairing or have significant navigation limitations. None were built for complete autonomy for the visually impaired.
→The problem was not just technical. It was about dignity and independence. The product had to work reliably enough that a user could trust it without any sighted assistance.
Approach
→Defined the full product requirements and system architecture: lane detection, object recognition, obstacle detection, and audio feedback — all integrated into a cart chassis with a built-in seat
→Built lane detection using OpenCV — perceptive transformation, threshold operations, and Canny edge detection to identify lane centers in real time at 90fps
→Trained Haar cascade ML models for object and obstacle recognition — the cart identifies product categories (fruits, vegetables, dairy) and announces them via text-to-speech using the eSpeak module
→Implemented Raspberry Pi (master) and Arduino Uno (slave) architecture — Raspberry Pi handles all image processing and ML inference, Arduino controls motor movement via PWM signals
→Iterated on edge cases: proximity thresholds (5-20cm detection range), low-light failure modes, obstacle rerouting logic, and FPS vs accuracy tradeoffs
Hardware and software in action
Outcome
→Fully functional prototype built and demonstrated — cart navigates lanes, identifies product categories, announces location via audio, and avoids obstacles autonomously
→Recognized at Yukti 2.0 by the Ministry of Education, Govt. of India for societal impact in assistive technology
→Research published in Springer (2023) — view paper ↗
→Built for under 12,000 INR — intentional cost constraint to ensure real-world accessibility and replicability
Takeaways
→Societal impact needs product discipline. A good cause is not enough. The cart had to be reliable enough that a visually impaired person could trust it without sighted backup.
→Edge cases are the product, especially in accessibility. The failure mode that affects 5% of users can be the one that makes the product unusable for the people who need it most.
→Constraints sharpen decisions. The 12,000 INR budget forced every component choice to be deliberate. Cost constraints in early prototyping often produce better design thinking than unlimited resources.
→Hardware is a product too. Defining what the system should do, for whom, and under what conditions — before touching code — was the most important step in building something that worked.
Product RequirementsSystem ArchitectureComputer VisionMachine LearningOpenCVRaspberry PiAccessibilityEdge Case TestingIoTDeep Neural NetworksPrototypingResearch Publication

// a side project with a name
The Social Snail
supper club · coming soon
Someday I'll open a supper club.
Until then, I build products.
The idea for The Social Snail came from something I've always loved: bringing together people from completely different parts of my life and seeing what happens.

My friends hate it. Every time I host, they walk in and ask, "Snehal, how do you even know these people?" But that's my favorite part.

I'll throw strangers into the same room and trust the process. Two hours later, they're debating the best biryani in town like it's a matter of national importance. The food was just the excuse.
Coming soon  ·  great food, interesting people, conversations that make you lose track of time
met for lunch · yes it was birthday themed
